reasoning: The sentence 'Valaki megvert valakit' has 'valaki' as subject (someone) and 'valakit' as object (someone accusative), with the verb 'megvert' (beat). This directly translates to 'Someone beat someone,' matching (H).

The sentence 'Senki nem verte meg a Pétert' has 'senki' subject (no one), negation 'nem', verb 'verte meg' (beat), object 'a Pétert' (Peter accusative). This translates to 'No one beat Peter,' matching (F). The word order emphasizes the subject 'senki'.

Assuming the 4th sentence is 'Senki nem vert meg mindekit' (based on context and pattern, labeled as N5 in text but sequential), 'senki' (no one subject), 'nem', verb, 'mindekit' (everyone accusative). Translates to 'No one beat everyone,' matching (A).

The sentence 'Senkit nem vert meg Péter' has 'senkit' (no one accusative, object focused), 'nem', verb, subject 'Péter' at end. This means 'Peter beat no one' or 'Peter didn't beat anyone,' matching (K) 'Peter's the one who didn't beat anyone.' Word order focuses on the object.

The sentence 'Ki nem vert meg senkit?' questions 'ki' (who subject?), 'nem', verb, 'senkit' (no one accusative object). Translates to 'Who didn't beat anyone?,' matching (J). The question word is in subject position.

The sentence 'Valakit nem vert meg senki' has 'valakit' (someone accusative, focused object), 'nem', verb, 'senki' (no one subject). Means 'No one beat someone' i.e. 'There’s someone who didn't get beaten,' matching (L). Focus on the object indicates existential scope.

The sentence 'Mindenki megvert valakit' has 'mindenki' (everyone subject), verb, 'valakit' (someone object). Translates to 'Everyone beat someone,' matching (I). Universal quantifier on subject with existential on object.
The sentence 'Valaki megverte a Marcit' has 'valaki' (someone subject), verb 'megverte' (beat, past), 'a Marcit' (Martin accusative). Translates to 'Someone beat Martin,' matching (D).

The sentence 'Senkit nem vert meg senki' focuses 'senkit' (no one accusative object), 'nem', verb, 'senki' (no one subject). This double negative structure translates to 'No one got beaten,' matching (C), as no one beat anyone.

The sentence 'Kit nem vert meg senki?' questions 'kit' (who object?), 'nem', verb, 'senki' (no one subject). Translates to 'Who did no one beat?' i.e. 'Who wasn't beaten by anyone?,' matching (B), assuming 'by anyone' contextually fits the negation for the unbeaten one.
The sentence 'Nem vertem meg senkit' has negation 'nem', verb 'vertem' (I beat, first person), 'senkit' (no one accusative). Translates to 'I didn't beat anyone,' matching (E).

Assuming the 13th sentence follows the pattern, likely a variant like 'Egyiket nem vert meg senki' or similar focused existential for the unbeaten, translating to 'There’s one who didn't get beaten,' matching (M).
